choices.prompt: According to the module, where does Application Insights land the telemetry it collects, and what is the consequence for how you query it?
choices: In a Log Analytics workspace, where every signal is queryable with KQL in the same place, so you can pivot from a metric spike to the exact failing requests without changing tools
In a dedicated metrics-only store, so traces and logs must be exported separately before they can be correlated
In Azure Key Vault, so that telemetry inherits the same secret-protection guarantees described in module 2
In per-service local log files that an engineer must grep across machines during an incident
correct_answers: In a Log Analytics workspace, where every signal is queryable with KQL in the same place, so you can pivot from a metric spike to the exact failing requests without changing tools

choices.prompt: The rate-quote API's own server-side duration looks healthy, but users report slow responses. Per the module, which telemetry signal and view should the engineer reach for?
choices: A distributed trace shown in the end-to-end transaction view, which breaks the request into per-dependency durations across services
Aggregated metrics on a latency dashboard, because p95 latency is the only way to see where time is spent
Raw logs filtered by severity, because each healthy request already records its full per-hop timing
An alert rule, because thresholds on the failure-rate metric reveal which downstream hop is slow
correct_answers: A distributed trace shown in the end-to-end transaction view, which breaks the request into per-dependency durations across services

choices.prompt: In the module's KQL model, what does the pipe symbol (|) express and how does a query read?
choices: It pipes rows through operators like Unix pipes, and the query reads top-to-bottom with each line transforming the rows produced by the line above
It combines two independent tables by joining them on a shared correlation ID before any filtering happens
It marks an alternative condition, so the engine evaluates each branch and returns whichever produces more rows
It denotes a comment boundary, separating the human-readable intent from the executable portion of the query
correct_answers: It pipes rows through operators like Unix pipes, and the query reads top-to-bottom with each line transforming the rows produced by the line above

choices.prompt: Which statements about distributed tracing and correlation, as described in the module, are correct? (Select all that apply.)
choices: Application Insights stitches a request into one trace by propagating a correlation identifier across each hop, largely automatically via auto-instrumentation and the W3C Trace Context standard
Each operation gets an operation ID, and child calls carry the parent's context so the backend can reassemble the tree
Bypassing the instrumented client or stripping headers loses trace-context propagation, so the dependency appears disconnected
Correlation is reconstructed after the fact by aggregating metrics over time, which is why traces are cheaper to store than metrics
correct_answers: Application Insights stitches a request into one trace by propagating a correlation identifier across each hop, largely automatically via auto-instrumentation and the W3C Trace Context standard
Each operation gets an operation ID, and child calls carry the parent's context so the backend can reassemble the tree
Bypassing the instrumented client or stripping headers loses trace-context propagation, so the dependency appears disconnected

choices.prompt: Which of the following are listed as common pitfalls in the module? (Select all that apply.)
choices: Logging everything at full volume instead of using adaptive sampling and capturing rich detail mainly on errors
Putting secrets in telemetry, such as logging full request bodies or connection strings
Collecting metrics but defining no alert rules, so telemetry is only watched on a dashboard nobody is looking at
Storing telemetry in a Log Analytics workspace instead of keeping each signal in its own purpose-built store
correct_answers: Logging everything at full volume instead of using adaptive sampling and capturing rich detail mainly on errors
Putting secrets in telemetry, such as logging full request bodies or connection strings
Collecting metrics but defining no alert rules, so telemetry is only watched on a dashboard nobody is looking at

choices.prompt: Per the module, how does it characterize metrics versus logs as telemetry signals?
choices: Metrics are numeric measurements aggregated over time, cheap to store and ideal for dashboards and alerts; logs are discrete, timestamped events with structured properties, ideal for what exactly happened to a request
Metrics are discrete timestamped events with structured properties, while logs are numeric values aggregated over time for dashboards
Metrics and logs are the same signal stored in different tables, distinguished only by which KQL operator queries them
Metrics record the full per-hop path of a single request, while logs only hold aggregated counts for alerting
correct_answers: Metrics are numeric measurements aggregated over time, cheap to store and ideal for dashboards and alerts; logs are discrete, timestamped events with structured properties, ideal for what exactly happened to a request

choices.prompt: You want an alert when the rate-quote service's failure rate exceeds a threshold over five minutes. According to the module, which signal underpins that alert and why not query logs directly for each evaluation?
choices: Metrics, because aggregated numeric measurements over time are cheap to evaluate continuously and are what alert rules are designed to threshold, whereas querying raw logs for every evaluation is expensive and slower
Logs, because each evaluation must re-read every individual request event to compute an accurate failure rate
Traces, because only the end-to-end transaction view can count failures across the five-minute window
Metrics, because logs cannot record whether a request succeeded or failed and so carry no failure information
correct_answers: Metrics, because aggregated numeric measurements over time are cheap to evaluate continuously and are what alert rules are designed to threshold, whereas querying raw logs for every evaluation is expensive and slower

choices.prompt: After calling AddApplicationInsightsTelemetry at startup, what does the module say auto-collection captures without per-call code?
choices: Requests, dependencies, and exceptions
Metrics, dashboards, and alert rules
Connection strings, request bodies, and Key Vault secrets
Correlation IDs, Unix pipes, and KQL projections

choices.prompt: Based on the module's KQL walkthrough, which statements about how the diagnostic queries are constructed are correct? (Select all that apply.)
choices: A query starts from a table such as requests, dependencies, exceptions, or traces and pipes it through operators
The dependencies query filters to the incident window and to success == false, then summarizes failure count and p95 duration by target and type
The requests query bins failure rate per minute over the hour, revealing a spike at 02:11 two minutes after the 02:09 deploy
The queries reconstruct the failing request's per-hop path by joining aggregated metrics, since traces are unavailable in KQL
correct_answers: A query starts from a table such as requests, dependencies, exceptions, or traces and pipes it through operators
The dependencies query filters to the incident window and to success == false, then summarizes failure count and p95 duration by target and type
The requests query bins failure rate per minute over the hour, revealing a spike at 02:11 two minutes after the 02:09 deploy

choices.prompt: Which practices does the module recommend regarding sensitive data and trace correlation in telemetry? (Select all that apply.)
choices: Scrub or exclude sensitive fields before they are recorded, because logging full request bodies or connection strings turns the observability store into a secret leak
Use the framework's instrumented HTTP clients so correlation headers flow automatically across each hop
Hardcode the Application Insights connection string in Program.cs so telemetry always has a guaranteed endpoint
Strip trace-context headers from downstream calls to reduce request size, since correlation can be rebuilt later from metrics
correct_answers: Scrub or exclude sensitive fields before they are recorded, because logging full request bodies or connection strings turns the observability store into a secret leak
Use the framework's instrumented HTTP clients so correlation headers flow automatically across each hop

llm.prompt: Explain why the module says you must match the telemetry signal to the question, contrasting metrics, logs, and traces. Give the kind of question each signal is best suited to answer.
llm.reference_answer: The module describes three signals, each best for a different kind of question. Metrics are numeric measurements aggregated over time (request rate, p95 latency, CPU) that are cheap to store and ideal for dashboards and alerts answering 'is something wrong and how much.' Logs are discrete, timestamped events with structured properties, ideal for 'what exactly happened to this request.' Traces connect the events of a single operation across service boundaries, ideal for 'where did the time go and which hop failed.' Confusing them wastes time: trying to reconstruct a single request's path from aggregated metrics, or trending latency by scanning raw logs, is the wrong tool each way, so you reach for the signal that matches the question.

llm.prompt: Walk through how the on-call engineer used Application Insights and KQL to diagnose the 02:14 failure, from instrumentation to root cause.
llm.reference_answer: First the team instruments the ASP.NET Core rate-quote API with a few lines at startup (AddApplicationInsightsTelemetry, with the connection string supplied via configuration or Key Vault, never hardcoded), so auto-collection captures requests, dependencies, and exceptions without per-call code. With telemetry flowing, the engineer opens Logs and runs a KQL query over the dependencies table filtered to the incident window and success == false, summarizing failure counts and p95 duration by target and type, which names the pricing-api HTTP dependency as the culprit. To confirm the trigger, they query the requests table, binning failure rate per minute, and see it spike at 02:11, two minutes after a 02:09 deploy of the pricing service. Finally the end-to-end transaction view for one failing request shows the exact timed-out call and its exception, making the fix (roll back the pricing deploy) obvious and evidenced, all in minutes.

llm.prompt: A teammate wrote a custom HTTP client for downstream calls, but those calls never appear linked to their parent request in the transaction view. Explain what most likely broke and how to fix it, grounding your answer in how distributed tracing works.
llm.reference_answer: Distributed tracing stitches a request into one trace by propagating a correlation identifier across each hop, relying on the W3C Trace Context standard where each operation gets an operation ID and child calls carry the parent's context. A hand-rolled HTTP client that bypasses the instrumented client or strips headers loses that trace-context propagation, so the downstream call has no parent correlation and appears disconnected in the transaction view. The fix is to use the framework's instrumented HTTP client so correlation headers flow automatically, or otherwise forward the trace-context headers, so context propagation is restored across the hop. The module lists this as a common pitfall: breaking trace correlation with hand-rolled HTTP calls.
